text:
REFINED BRIEFING (events grouped by probative direction).
Retrieval hints: actors=['Kash Patel', 'FBI Director', 'U.S. Senate', 'Senate Judiciary Committee', 'President Trump'], topics=['fbi', 'nomination', 'confirmation', 'senate', 'white house'].

## BACKGROUND (12)
- [2025-01-23] The U.S. Senate confirmed John Ratcliffe as the 25th Director of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) on Thursday, January 23, 2025, with a 74-25 vote. Vice President JD Vance swore Ratcliffe into office approximately two hours later at the White House.

Ratcliffe previously served as Director of National Intelligence (DNI) during President Trump's first term, becoming the first individual to hold both the DNI and CIA Director positions. He also served as a U.S. congressman from Texas.

During his January 15, 2025, confirmation hearing, Ratcliffe outlined plans for the CIA to be more aggressive in collecting human intelligence and countering U.S. adversaries. Some Democrats, including Senators Ron Wyden and Chuck Schumer, opposed his nomination, citing concerns about partisanship, his experience, and his ability to challenge the administration.
- [2025-01-14] Senate confirmation hearings for President-elect Donald Trump's Cabinet nominees are scheduled to commence on Tuesday, January 14, 2025. Thirteen nominees are slated to appear before 11 different Senate committees throughout the week. Pete Hegseth, Trump’s nominee for defense secretary, will have his hearing before the Senate Armed Services Committee on Tuesday morning, where allegations of misconduct and questions regarding his qualifications are expected to be raised. Pam Bondi, the attorney general nominee, is scheduled to appear before the Senate Judiciary Committee on Wednesday and Thursday, where her independence from Trump is expected to be a key topic. Marco Rubio, nominated for secretary of state, and John Ratcliffe, nominated for CIA director, also have hearings on Wednesday. Tulsi Gabbard, the DNI nominee, is expected to have her hearing next week, while Kash Patel, the FBI director nominee, is slated for February. Democrats are anticipated to scrutinize certain nominees, potentially prolonging the confirmation process.
- [2025-01-16] Senate confirmation hearings for President-elect Donald Trump's Cabinet nominees continued for a third day on Thursday, January 16, 2025.

Hearings began at 10 a.m. Eastern for Doug Burgum, nominated for Interior Secretary; Scott Turner, nominated for Housing and Urban Development Secretary; and Lee Zeldin, nominated for Environmental Protection Agency Administrator. Scott Bessent, nominated for Treasury Secretary, was scheduled for 10:30 a.m. Pam Bondi continued her hearing for Attorney General, with senators hearing from witnesses.

Bessent is a South Carolina billionaire. Burgum, a former North Dakota governor, is slated to chair a new National Energy Council. Turner served in the first Trump administration.

Earlier in the week, Pete Hegseth had a hearing for Defense Secretary, and Marco Rubio for Secretary of State on Wednesday. Kristi Noem, nominated for Homeland Security Secretary, is scheduled for a hearing on Friday.
- [2025-01-15] Six of President-elect Donald Trump's cabinet nominees are undergoing confirmation hearings on Capitol Hill. Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso expressed expectations for swift approval and bipartisan cooperation. Pete Hegseth, Trump's nominee for Secretary of Defense, was questioned in a hearing on January 14, 2025. Senator Joni Ernst publicly supported Hegseth. Florida Senator Marco Rubio was nominated for Secretary of State, and former Congressman Sean Duffy was nominated for Transportation Secretary. Pam Bondi, Trump's nominee for Attorney General, is expected to face questioning from Democrats regarding her past loyalty to Trump and her involvement in efforts to challenge the 2020 election results. Senate rules require a simple majority for confirmation, and Republicans hold 53 votes.
- [2025-01-25] Pete Hegseth was sworn in as the US Defence Secretary on Saturday, January 25, 2025, following his confirmation by the Senate. His nomination was approved following a 50-50 Senate vote, with Vice-President JD Vance casting the tie-breaking vote on Friday night, January 24, 2025. Three Republican senators—Mitch McConnell, Lisa Murkowski, and Susan Collins—voted against him. Vance's action marked only the second time in US history a vice-president broke a tie to confirm a cabinet nominee. Hegseth, 44, a former combat veteran, will now lead the Department of Defense, which manages approximately three million employees and an $849 billion budget. During his confirmation hearing, Hegseth faced questions regarding allegations of sexual assault, infidelity, and excessive drinking, all of which he denied.
- [2025-01-23] The U.S. Senate advanced President Trump’s nomination of Pete Hegseth as Secretary of Defense on Thursday with a 51-49 vote. Senators Lisa Murkowski of Alaska and Susan Collins of Maine, both Republicans, voted against advancing the nomination. Hegseth, a former combat veteran and Fox News host, faces allegations of excessive drinking, sexual assault, and other aggressive actions toward women, which he has denied. Concerns were also raised about his qualifications and past statements regarding women in military combat roles. A final confirmation vote is anticipated for Friday. The Senate had previously confirmed Marco Rubio as Secretary of State and John Ratcliffe as CIA director.
- [2025-01-24] Pete Hegseth was confirmed as defense secretary by the Senate in a 50-49 vote on Friday, January 24, 2025. Vice President J.D. Vance cast a tie-breaking vote.

Senators Lisa Murkowski, Susan Collins, and Mitch McConnell, all Republicans, voted against Hegseth. Hegseth's nomination drew scrutiny over his qualifications and allegations of misconduct. Hegseth denied allegations of sexual assault and abuse. He previously paid $50,000 to a woman in connection with one claim. During the confirmation process, he adjusted previous statements regarding women in military combat roles.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune, a Republican, supported Hegseth, a former Fox News host and combat veteran. Thune stated Hegseth would bring a "warrior culture" to the Pentagon and shift its focus to "war fighting" from diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives.

Hegseth will lead a department with 2.1 million service members, 780,000 civilians, and an $850 billion budget.
- [2025-01-20] The U.S. Senate confirmed Marco Rubio as Secretary of State on Monday, making him the first member of President Donald Trump's new Cabinet. The vote was unanimous, 99-0, on Inauguration Day. Rubio, a Republican senator from Florida, had his nomination advanced by the Senate Foreign Relations Committee earlier on Monday. As Secretary of State, Rubio would serve as the nation's top diplomat and would be the first Latino to hold the position. He has a background in foreign affairs, particularly concerning South America and China.
